name: TRIIM reported lower epigenetic age in an uncontrolled human pilot
summary: The TRIIM pilot is a notable human epigenetic-clock signal, but its uncontrolled, small, male-only design limits interpretation.
statement: In nine healthy men, one year of the TRIIM thymus-regeneration regimen was associated with lower DNA-methylation epigenetic age and immunosenescence-marker movement.
time_horizon: One year of treatment plus reported six-month follow-up for GrimAge persistence
quantitative_note: The abstract reports mean epigenetic age about 1.5 years below baseline after one year and a -2.5-year change compared with no treatment at study end.
caveats: The study was uncontrolled and included only nine men.
The regimen combined growth hormone, DHEA, metformin, vitamin D, and zinc, so the active component is unclear.
Several authors had Intervene Immune equity, roles, or related patent interests.
The result is a biomarker signal, not evidence of durable functional, disease, or mortality benefit.
